i respectfully disagree. i think barthod is quickly entering that ‘elite desirable producer’ group pretty quickly. its all relative but you could find lesser vintages and lesser 1er parcels for sub $100 just a year or two ago… now those seem to be going for $125-175. cras maybe for $125-150 a year or two ago and now, $200-300 for top vintages?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
For me, Beaux Bruns is the most open and youthfully demonstrative (which, for Barthod, means drink age 20 rather than age 30). Combottes is also a comparatively fleshy, giving wine, from a warm site, but not to the extent of Beaux Bruns. Baudes is a baby Bonnes-Mares, with deep fruit and in recent vintages plenty of ripe tannin. Charmes is a bit ampler and more enveloping, a very complete wine. Cras and Fuées are both superb: Cras a bit chalkier in tannic profile, Fuées more seamless young. Often I prefer Véroilles to anything, however - a special site, a few steps away from the d’Auvenay Bonnes-Mares (another of my favorite wines in contemporary Burgundy).

These wines have been great for a long time, and it seems the market is finally realizing that you don’t have to have Amoureuses, Bonnes-Mares or Musigny to make some of the best wines in Chambolle-Musigny and indeed all of Burgundy. They are, however, slow burns; and I would rather open e.g. a 2000 Beaux Bruns today than a 2010 Cras, even if the Cras is the “better” wine of the two.

posts.name: Matthew King
posts.cooked:
Pithy analysis William.

Given her structured style, I tend to like her “lesser” vineyards in “lesser” years for near-term consumption.

Her Bourgogne is also just the ticket whilst waiting for her 1er crus to thaw. I think her Bon Batons is one of my three favorite Bourgognes made regionwide.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: 07 AC was drinking well this year.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Pithy analysis William.

Given her structured style, I tend to like her “lesser” vineyards in “lesser” years for near-term consumption.

Her Bourgogne is also just the ticket whilst waiting for her 1er crus to thaw. I think her Bon Batons is one of my three favorite Bourgognes made regionwide.

It’s definitely in the conversation! And I should have said, really, that the regional and communal bottlings are superb without even getting into the premiers crus.

I remember that, as a student, my first encounter with a Barthod wine was a 2001, at the time very tight and unfriendly; and it took me a while, and the generosity of a few friends with mature bottles, to really understand what the estate is all about. Today, I’d rank this among my favorite estates in the region.

I drank Cras ‘03 back during 2016. The breezes through the Combe and higher clay content in soil seemed to maintain freshness of wine even given the extreme conditions of vintage. Delicious bottle.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: She makes beautiful, pure wines from top to bottom. They do indeed require substantial aging to blossom.

Buy a single bottle of each and decide for yourself.

All of the wines are good - I started buying them 19 vintages ago. I’ve developed some preferences over time, but they are more personal than anything else…

William is right to point out that they require long cellar time to really shine.

Note: it may be a bit more complex to do the assessment in a ripe vintage like 2018 - as the ripeness may mask a bit of the terroir.

-mark

PS. To me, Cras isn’t the Domaines best terroir…I get more minerality and terroir in another bottling.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked: Fuees is my favorite.

Thanks for the insights, might be going for some more 1ers (Baudes or Bruns) if available for a good price!

I’ve got you beat by a decade! The backstory here is that I was a young wine person and Josh Raynolds, then the Rosenthal rep for our area, stopped by and tasted us on the 1992 Barthod Chambolle and Bernard Amiot Charmes*. This was Baltimore, the heart of Parker country, and I had always been told that “Burgundy was a minefield” and we carried Burgundy but sold much more Bordeaux, CA, Chateauneuf, etc… Well, these wines floored me (1992 was a “weaker” vintage but showed really well young and into the early 2000s, I drank many bottles of Mugneret Ruchottes at Tahn Dihn during that time for like $60) and woke me up to the grace and beauty that were possible here from pinot noir and inspired me to visit year after year.

Anyway, it’s not the best of her terroir, but I find myself always loving the Charmes for it’s seductiveness relative to the more intellectual qualities of my other favorite Crus. Mark, I think I know which you think is the best and I probably agree.

\

  • These were lovely wines that don’t exist anymore, I forget who got the holdings (maybe William remembers). In style, they were like a better version of Truchot but in Chambolle, richer than Truchot but without sacrificing any clarity. Seamless and digestible.

I’ve got you beat by a decade! The backstory here is that I was a young wine person and Josh Raynolds, then the Rosenthal rep for our area, stopped by and tasted us on the 1992 Barthod Chambolle and Bernard Amiot Charmes*. This was Baltimore, the heart of Parker country, and I had always been told that “Burgundy was a minefield” and we carried Burgundy but sold much more Bordeaux, CA, Chateauneuf, etc… Well, these wines floored me (1992 was a “weaker” vintage but showed really well young and into the early 2000s, I drank many bottles of Mugneret Ruchottes at Tahn Dihn during that time for like $60) and woke me up to the grace and beauty that were possible here from pinot noir and inspired me to visit year after year.

Anyway, it’s not the best of her terroir, but I find myself always loving the Charmes for it’s seductiveness relative to the more intellectual qualities of my other favorite Crus. Mark, I think I know which you think is the best and I probably agree.

\

  • These were lovely wines that don’t exist anymore, I forget who got the holdings (maybe William remembers). In style, they were like a better version of Truchot but in Chambolle, richer than Truchot but without sacrificing any clarity. Seamless and digestible.

I think that the idea that Charmes is some sort of second tier premier cru ought to be resisted. It’s large, so not all sites are created equal and there are necessarily many producers, not all of them good; but great Charmes is deep, rather sumptuous, very complete, and notably age worthy. If a Chambolle Charmes isn’t good, it isn’t because of the terroir!

Cool story…Didn’t think you were that old, VLM~Nathan! Oldest I’ve had is the '93 Cras and Fuees a few times and I drank them all too soon…I love the style of the Charmes - the range really goes from strength to strength. Don’t recall ever having a Bernard Amiot Charmes…better version of Truchot - sounds ethereal and elegant - sounds good to me. One of these days we’ll have to do a Barthod-a-thon.

Just picked up some 18s to try - it will be interesting to taste how domain managed the ripeness of the vintage…I hope like Lafarge… -mark

Well, I was 22-23 when I started. I had some wines from the 80s that were made by Gaston. IMO, the wines made by Ghislaine have been much better, but it was a different time then and lots of Burgundy were more rustic.

We should definitely do a Barthod-a-thon. I have a few single bottles left from the mid-90s so we better do it soon before I drink them!

Lafarge is a good comp in a lot of ways. I think that she has managed to keep the distinctions between vineyards even in warm years. I’m waiting for my allocation of 2018s from my local Rosenthal purveyor. I generally don’t open them on release, I squirrel them away for a few years. I recently had a 2014 Cras that was surprisingly approachable which is a nice devlopement, IMO.

posts.name: Anders G
posts.cooked:
I had my Barthod „Epiphany“ with the 1996s. I love the purity of the wines, also in their youth before they close down. They do close down hard however as many have mentioned before. I normally like my Burgundy in the transition phase, when the fruit is still somewhat primary, the wine still has edges but starts smoothing out. But not here. I don‘t touch neither the Villages nor the Crus before they have reached age 20. A 1999 Fuées recently was absolutely gorgeous and on highest level.

The price level of Ghislaines wines has always been somewhat high, at least in my market. With the prices generally rising in Burgundy they have indeed become quite expensive - as many others too.

My favorite Crus are the Véroilles and the Fuées, which together with the Villages are the ones I used to buy.

posts.name: Carl Steefel
posts.cooked: Had the 2002 Barthod Cras next to a 1999 Mugnier Fuees a couple of years ago. I think the wine was 15 years at the time, but still showing some baby fat, perhaps reflecting the big vintage. In retrospect, this would have been better at close to 20, but still impressive for its richness and depth, even if missing the minerality and complexity of the 1999 Fuees. But a few extra years could have made a big difference…

Had the 2002 Barthod Cras next to a 1999 Mugnier Fuees a couple of years ago. I think the wine was 15 years at the time, but still showing some baby fat, perhaps reflecting the big vintage. In retrospect, this would have been better at close to 20, but still impressive for its richness and depth, even if missing the minerality and complexity of the 1999 Fuees. But a few extra years could have made a big difference…

That note speaks more to the vintage and vineyard than the producers. '99 Barthod Fuees is great, and often the wine I find is most mineral amongst the portfolio, but 10-20 years young.

-mark

posts.name: Jon Arvid Rosengren
posts.cooked:
Pithy analysis William.

Given her structured style, I tend to like her “lesser” vineyards in “lesser” years for near-term consumption.

Her Bourgogne is also just the ticket whilst waiting for her 1er crus to thaw. I think her Bon Batons is one of my three favorite Bourgognes made regionwide.

It’s definitely in the conversation! And I should have said, really, that the regional and communal bottlings are superb without even getting into the premiers crus.

I remember that, as a student, my first encounter with a Barthod wine was a 2001, at the time very tight and unfriendly; and it took me a while, and the generosity of a few friends with mature bottles, to really understand what the estate is all about. Today, I’d rank this among my favorite estates in the region.

I have a remarkably parallel story with Bartod. I, along with a sommelier friend in Copenhagen, had saved up some money and courage to order a bottle 2001 Barthod (I believe it was Beaux Bruns but 10+ years later I’m not so sure) which we had oogled over on a wine list for months. It think it was 1200 DKK at the time, or around $200 in today’s terms. I remember it as a very disappointing experience. It was so tight and mean, and we really, really wanted it to be good. C’est la vie. Today I very much appreciate her wines, with a better understanding of vintages and drinking windows.

Strangely, $200 seems like a lot in 2010-ish terms, given that we drank villages and lesser 1er Cru Coche, d’Auvenay, Engel, Rousseau, Truchot etc. at this same establishment for less. Very anecdotal argument against Barthod rising faster than others.

Anyone here try the '17 bathod cras?

i think michael chang has had one or 42 of them.

haha I haven’t had any of mine yet; i don’t tend to open barthod 1er until at least 10 years after the vintage date.

10 years is optimistic.’

I’m still working through Bourgogne from 16 years ago! (2005)!

-mark

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Pithy analysis William.

Given her structured style, I tend to like her “lesser” vineyards in “lesser” years for near-term consumption.

Her Bourgogne is also just the ticket whilst waiting for her 1er crus to thaw. I think her Bon Batons is one of my three favorite Bourgognes made regionwide.

It’s definitely in the conversation! And I should have said, really, that the regional and communal bottlings are superb without even getting into the premiers crus.

I remember that, as a student, my first encounter with a Barthod wine was a 2001, at the time very tight and unfriendly; and it took me a while, and the generosity of a few friends with mature bottles, to really understand what the estate is all about. Today, I’d rank this among my favorite estates in the region.

I have a remarkably parallel story with Bartod. I, along with a sommelier friend in Copenhagen, had saved up some money and courage to order a bottle 2001 Barthod (I believe it was Beaux Bruns but 10+ years later I’m not so sure) which we had oogled over on a wine list for months. It think it was 1200 DKK at the time, or around $200 in today’s terms. I remember it as a very disappointing experience. It was so tight and mean, and we really, really wanted it to be good. C’est la vie. Today I very much appreciate her wines, with a better understanding of vintages and drinking windows.

Strangely, $200 seems like a lot in 2010-ish terms, given that we drank villages and lesser 1er Cru Coche, d’Auvenay, Engel, Rousseau, Truchot etc. at this same establishment for less. Very anecdotal argument against Barthod rising faster than others.

I have a similar story. Some years ago, I took a good friend to dinner at a very nice restaurant and we ordered a bottle of a 2005 Barthod 1er cru (I think it was Fuees, but may have been Cras). It was a genuinely painful experience that was also not cheap (at a time when I also had less money and was spending a lot less on wine).

Personally, I’ve yet to have a breakthrough moment with Barthod. Hope springs eternal. As far as pricing, I’ve been buying smatterings of Barthod since 2010 (when I was supposed to get a magnum of Cras that was sadly broken during shipment from the warehouse), and I don’t think the pricing has risen out of line with other producers at all. Compared to other traditional producers like Lafarge, for example, the price increases have seemed slower to me.

C’est la vie. Today I very much appreciate her wines, with a better understanding of vintages and drinking windows.

Strangely, $200 seems like a lot in 2010-ish terms, given that we drank villages and lesser 1er Cru Coche, d’Auvenay, Engel, Rousseau, Truchot etc. at this same establishment for less. Very anecdotal argument against Barthod rising faster than others.

I have a similar story. Some years ago, I took a good friend to dinner at a very nice restaurant and we ordered a bottle of a 2005 Barthod 1er cru (I think it was Fuees, but may have been Cras). It was a genuinely painful experience that was also not cheap (at a time when I also had less money and was spending a lot less on wine).

Personally, I’ve yet to have a breakthrough moment with Barthod. Hope springs eternal. As far as pricing, I’ve been buying smatterings of Barthod since 2010 (when I was supposed to get a magnum of Cras that was sadly broken during shipment from the warehouse), and I don’t think the pricing has risen out of line with other producers at all. Compared to other traditional producers like Lafarge, for example, the price increases have seemed slower to me.

Agree. My mind set for these wines is the Old Gouges camp. You are going to have to wait. Plus or minus. And you will catch many in the grumpy phase if impatient.

I suspect with the ultimate (imminent?) consolidation of her Domaine and her husband’s under Clément, more hype and a consequent permanent price jump in the near future would not be surprising. Some serious old vines sites in prime locations in the combined domaine.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
I have a remarkably parallel story with Bartod. I, along with a sommelier friend in Copenhagen, had saved up some money and courage to order a bottle 2001 Barthod (I believe it was Beaux Bruns but 10+ years later I’m not so sure) which we had oogled over on a wine list for months. It think it was 1200 DKK at the time, or around $200 in today’s terms. I remember it as a very disappointing experience. It was so tight and mean, and we really, really wanted it to be good. C’est la vie. Today I very much appreciate her wines, with a better understanding of vintages and drinking windows.

Strangely, $200 seems like a lot in 2010-ish terms, given that we drank villages and lesser 1er Cru Coche, d’Auvenay, Engel, Rousseau, Truchot etc. at this same establishment for less. Very anecdotal argument against Barthod rising faster than others.

Personally, I’ve yet to have a breakthrough moment with Barthod. Hope springs eternal. As far as pricing, I’ve been buying smatterings of Barthod since 2010 (when I was supposed to get a magnum of Cras that was sadly broken during shipment from the warehouse), and I don’t think the pricing has risen out of line with other producers at all. Compared to other traditional producers like Lafarge, for example, the price increases have seemed slower to me.

Agree. My mind set for these wines is the Old Gouges camp. You are going to have to wait. Plus or minus. And you will catch many in the grumpy phase if impatient.

I suspect with the ultimate (imminent?) consolidation of her Domaine and her husband’s under Clément, more hype and a consequent permanent price jump in the near future would not be surprising. Some serious old vines sites in prime locations in the combined domaine.

I’ve wondered the same given how much Roumier’s Cras commands. I’ve also always wondered what she might be able to do with a 1er VV cuvée a la Lignier. I realize suggesting blending is often anathema in Burgundy, but it’d certainly be fun from my perspective:)

posts.cooked:
Is Lignier blending the best of his 1ers in the vv? I suppose it’s more expensive than his other 1ers.

I assume drouhin does the opposite with theirs.

posts.name: Y∅ñg £êë
posts.cooked:
I’ve got an 08 Lignier VV in the cellar. Any ideas on when to open?

The bottle I had last was ready but it all depends on when you like to drink your wines. I prefer wines when it’s transitioning from 2ndry to tertiary phase or early in the tertiary phase.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Is Lignier blending the best of his 1ers in the vv? I suppose it’s more expensive than his other 1ers.

I assume drouhin does the opposite with theirs.

Well, blend is all relative - my understanding is that the two plots from which Lignier blends the VV are contiguous. But it’s not a tiny plot, and one of those two plots contains the oldest 1er cru vines the estate has, yes.

posts.name: S_Yæt€s
posts.cooked: There was a long discussion here about the Lignier a year or two ago, and at about the same time a shop here went out of business, closing out the '14 at about $100. I was surprised it was as overtly oaky as it was…getting the grand cru treatment. Is it always like that?

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
There was a long discussion here about the Lignier a year or two ago, and at about the same time a shop here went out of business, closing out the '14 at about $100. I was surprised it was as overtly oaky as it was…getting the grand cru treatment. Is it always like that?

I’m not sure what you mean by “grand cru treatment”, but it generally sees up to 30% new oak. Which, for an old vine MSD 1er cru seems reasonable to me. I’m fairly oak averse, and I don’t find the wines oaky.

posts.name: A. So
posts.cooked:
Is Lignier blending the best of his 1ers in the vv? I suppose it’s more expensive than his other 1ers.

Well, blend is all relative - my understanding is that the two plots from which Lignier blends the VV are contiguous. But it’s not a tiny plot, and one of those two plots contains the oldest 1er cru vines the estate has, yes.

Yep, Chenevery + Faconnieres. There is also a single vineyard Chenevery bottling which consists of the young vines. The old vines go into the 1er VV.

To really know your bottle of wine- even the same wine from the same producer as each vintage is different - one needs to open the wine periodically to taste it and experience it.

posts.name: S_Yæt€s
posts.cooked: BTW Greg Rosenthal’s site still says “approximately 20% to ­30% on the village wines and up to 50% for the Premier and Grand Crus. The wines of the village appellations usually spend 18 months in barrel while the Premier and Grand Crus remain in cask for 20 to 24 months”. Other references indicate less. Wonder if the younger generation has reduced this some.

link_counts.title: Domaine Hubert Lignier – Rosenthal Wine Merchant

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
There was a long discussion here about the Lignier a year or two ago, and at about the same time a shop here went out of business, closing out the '14 at about $100. I was surprised it was as overtly oaky as it was…getting the grand cru treatment. Is it always like that?

I’m not sure what you mean by “grand cru treatment”, but it generally sees up to 30% new oak. Which, for an old vine MSD 1er cru seems reasonable to me. I’m fairly oak averse, and I don’t find the wines oaky.

That is exactly what I was wondering…this bottle must have been an outlier if only 30% new oak is used.

Not sure what to tell you. I haven’t been drinking my 14s, but of the wines made by Laurent, I’ve never gotten much of an oak signature (I actually sometimes find Hubert’s wines slightly oaky even though they’re obviously older). I think Laurent made some of the best 16s in all of Burgundy and they don’t show oak to me, including the grand crus. His are also some of the very few 18s are buying; I liked them in barrel.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
BTW Greg Rosenthal’s site still says “approximately 20% to ­30% on the village wines and up to 50% for the Premier and Grand Crus. The wines of the village appellations usually spend 18 months in barrel while the Premier and Grand Crus remain in cask for 20 to 24 months”. Other references indicate less. Wonder if the younger generation has reduced this some.

Yeah, but take a look at the tech sheets on the website for the 1ers vs the grand crus. The “up to” is really for the grand crus. Which is also consistent with what Laurent says on visits. I think Adrien and Charlie have also visited Laurent, they can chime in too.

At any rate, this is slightly academic; the perception of oak isn’t really measurable. If a wine tastes oaky to your palate, then that’s what it tastes like. I have (very friendly) arguments with a fellow member of this forum about whether or not Fourrier’s village is so pretty in part due to the use of sexy oak, a la Comte Liger-Belair. I don’t think so (and Jean Marie says he uses neutral oak for the village wines), but if people taste oak and like the wines, who am I to argue?

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked: I never got that Lignier MSD VV was a super blend but more, as is the tradition, a blend of 2 vineyards because they were complementary and probably not enough to release separately.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I never got that Lignier MSD VV was a super blend but more, as is the tradition, a blend of 2 vineyards because they were complementary and probably not enough to release separately.

It’s actually one single parcel of optimally-situated very old vines that straddles two premiers crus.

posts.cooked:

yes I got that. My point is it’s not a select parcel of a wine they already release. Like Fourrier with the CSJ or Ligniers CSLR.
